stem:
A 78-year-old woman is seen in your general practice with a new diagnosis of persistent atrial fibrillation, picked up on routine ECG when she presented with palpitations. She has hypertension on perindopril and amlodipine, type 2 diabetes on metformin, and chronic kidney disease stage 3a (eGFR 52 mL/min per 1.73 m^2). She has never had a stroke or TIA. She lives independently and has no falls history.

Her CHA2DS2-VA score is 4 (hypertension, age 75 or over, diabetes, female sex). HAS-BLED is 1 (hypertension). She has no contraindications to oral anticoagulation. She is rate-controlled on metoprolol 50 mg twice daily and has chosen a rate-control strategy after counselling.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 78 /min, blood pressure 138/82 mmHg, respiratory rate 16 /min, SpO2 98% on room air, temperature 36.6 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: irregularly irregular pulse, no carotid bruits, no peripheral oedema, no signs of heart failure
data_table.Investigations: creatinine 102 micromol/L (45 to 90); eGFR 52 mL/min per 1.73 m^2; FBC and LFT within normal limits; ECG confirms AF rate 78

lead_in: What is the most appropriate anticoagulant choice?

options.text: Dabigatran 150 mg twice daily orally for stroke prevention
options.rationale: Dabigatran is highly renally cleared (80%) and is dose-adjusted in CKD. While usable here, apixaban is preferred in moderate CKD due to lower renal dependence and better bleeding profile in elderly patients. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ AF Guidelines)
source_refs: National Heart Foundation/CSANZ Atrial Fibrillation Guidelines

options.text: Aspirin 100 mg daily as antiplatelet stroke prevention monotherapy
options.rationale: Aspirin is no longer recommended for stroke prevention in atrial fibrillation. It has minimal benefit and similar bleeding risk to anticoagulants. NHFA/CSANZ explicitly removes aspirin from AF stroke prevention. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ AF Guidelines)
source_refs: National Heart Foundation/CSANZ Atrial Fibrillation Guidelines

options.text: Warfarin titrated to a therapeutic INR of 2.0 to 3.0
options.rationale: Warfarin is acceptable when DOACs are contraindicated, but DOACs (particularly apixaban) are preferred first-line in this setting given non-inferior efficacy, lower intracranial haemorrhage, and no INR monitoring. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ AF Guidelines)
source_refs: National Heart Foundation/CSANZ Atrial Fibrillation Guidelines

options.text: Apixaban 5 mg twice daily orally indefinitely for stroke prevention
options.rationale: Apixaban is the preferred DOAC in moderate CKD (eGFR 30 to 50) under NHFA/CSANZ AF guidance because of its lower renal clearance (about 27%) and a strong real-world safety profile. Standard dose 5 mg twice daily applies; dose reduction to 2.5 mg twice daily requires two of: age 80 or over, weight 60 kg or less, creatinine 133 micromol/L or higher. She meets none. (Source: National Heart Foundation/CSANZ Atrial Fibrillation Guidelines)
source_refs: National Heart Foundation/CSANZ Atrial Fibrillation Guidelines

options.text: Left atrial appendage closure with a Watchman device alone
options.rationale: LAA closure is reserved for patients with contraindications to long-term oral anticoagulation. She has no contraindications and tolerates oral therapy. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ AF Guidelines)
source_refs: National Heart Foundation/CSANZ Atrial Fibrillation Guidelines

explanation.summary: The NHFA/CSANZ Atrial Fibrillation Guidelines recommend oral anticoagulation for non-valvular AF when the CHA2DS2-VA score is 2 or higher. DOACs are preferred over warfarin in non-valvular AF for non-inferior efficacy, lower intracranial haemorrhage, and no INR monitoring. Apixaban is the preferred DOAC in moderate CKD (eGFR 30 to 50). Apixaban dose reduction (2.5 mg twice daily) requires two of: age 80 or over, weight 60 kg or less, creatinine 133 micromol/L or higher. Aspirin is no longer recommended for AF stroke prevention; LAA closure is reserved for those with anticoagulation contraindications.
key_points: CHA2DS2-VA 2 or higher: oral anticoagulation indicated; DOAC preferred over warfarin
Apixaban is preferred in moderate CKD (eGFR 30 to 50)
Apixaban 2.5 mg twice daily if two of: age 80 or over, weight 60 kg or less, creatinine 133 or higher
Aspirin is removed from AF stroke prevention; LAA closure if anticoagulation contraindicated
explanation.pearls: AF stroke prevention NHFA/CSANZ: CHA2DS2-VA 2 or higher gets a DOAC, preferably apixaban in moderate CKD (eGFR 30 to 50). Reduce apixaban to 2.5 mg twice daily if two of: age 80 or over, weight 60 kg or less, creatinine 133 or higher.

sources.label: National Heart Foundation/CSANZ Atrial Fibrillation Guidelines

stem:
A 72-year-old man with chronic kidney disease stage 4 is brought to the emergency department feeling weak and lethargic. His usual medications include perindopril, spironolactone, and atorvastatin. He had gastroenteritis with diarrhoea for three days and has been off food and water but kept taking his usual medications. He is alert and oriented but mildly confused.

His ECG shows peaked T waves in the precordial leads, a PR interval of 240 ms, and a QRS duration of 116 ms. He has no chest pain. He has not yet been catheterised.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 56 /min, blood pressure 132/78 mmHg, respiratory rate 16 /min, SpO2 98% on room air, temperature 36.7 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: mildly dehydrated, no peripheral oedema, no focal neurology
data_table.Investigations: potassium 7.2 mmol/L (3.5 to 5.0); sodium 138 mmol/L; bicarbonate 14 mmol/L; creatinine 380 micromol/L (baseline 220); pH 7.28; ECG: peaked T waves, PR 240 ms, QRS 116 ms

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

options.text: Urgent haemodialysis booking without further pharmacological intervention
options.rationale: Dialysis is the definitive treatment for refractory hyperkalaemia in CKD but takes time to set up. Pharmacological bridging with calcium gluconate then insulin-dextrose and salbutamol is started while dialysis is being arranged. (Source: KHA Hyperkalaemia)

options.text: IV actrapid insulin 10 units in 50 mL of 50% dextrose over 15 minutes
options.rationale: Insulin-dextrose lowers serum potassium by intracellular shift over 30 to 60 minutes but does not stabilise the myocardium acutely. With ECG changes, calcium gluconate comes first to protect against arrhythmia while waiting for shift therapy. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology; KHA)

options.text: Oral sodium polystyrene sulfonate (resonium) 30 g now and repeat in 4 hours
options.rationale: Cation-exchange resins act over hours and do not address acute arrhythmia risk. They are adjunctive for ongoing potassium load, not immediate. (Source: KHA Hyperkalaemia)

options.text: IV furosemide 80 mg as a bolus to drive renal potassium loss
options.rationale: Loop diuretics increase renal potassium excretion but require adequate renal function and time. With CKD stage 4, AKI on top, and dehydration, the response is limited and slow. Cardiac protection is the priority. (Source: KHA Hyperkalaemia)

options.text: IV calcium gluconate 10% 10 mL over 10 minutes for cardiac membrane stabilisation
options.rationale: ECG changes (peaked T waves, prolonged PR, widened QRS) in the setting of hyperkalaemia mandate immediate cardiac membrane stabilisation with IV calcium gluconate 10% 10 mL over 10 minutes. Calcium does not lower potassium but reduces myocardial excitability and arrhythmia risk within minutes. Insulin-dextrose and salbutamol then drive potassium intracellularly; definitive removal (diuretics, dialysis) follows. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology; KHA Hyperkalaemia)

explanation.summary: Acute hyperkalaemia with ECG changes is a medical emergency. The Kidney Health Australia stepwise approach is: immediate IV calcium gluconate 10% 10 mL over 10 minutes for membrane stabilisation, then intracellular shift therapy (IV insulin 10 units with dextrose 25 g; nebulised salbutamol 10 to 20 mg; sodium bicarbonate if acidotic), then definitive removal (diuretics if responsive, cation-exchange resins, or haemodialysis). The offending medications (ACE inhibitor, spironolactone) are held. Recheck potassium and ECG at 30 minutes; calcium gluconate can be repeated.
key_points: Calcium gluconate 10% 10 mL over 10 minutes for cardiac membrane stabilisation
Insulin-dextrose, salbutamol nebs, bicarbonate (if acidotic) drive intracellular shift
Diuretics, resins, or dialysis for definitive removal
Hold ACE inhibitors, ARBs, and potassium-sparing diuretics
explanation.pearls: Hyperkalaemia with ECG changes: IV calcium gluconate 10% 10 mL over 10 minutes for membrane stabilisation FIRST, then insulin-dextrose plus nebulised salbutamol for shift, then diuretics/resin/dialysis for removal. Hold ACEi, ARBs, spironolactone.

stem:
A 56-year-old man with type 2 diabetes attends the emergency department late at night with worsening left lower leg pain over 18 hours. He gave himself a small cut while gardening yesterday. The pain is now out of proportion to the small wound and he is reluctant to move his foot. He has had rigors at home and one episode of vomiting.

On examination there is a 1 cm puncture wound on the calf with surrounding erythema. The skin overlying the lateral calf is dusky purple in one area and tender well beyond the visible margin of erythema. There are no crepitations on palpation; an early plain film shows no gas. He is unwell-looking and rigoring on the trolley.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 122 /min, blood pressure 98/62 mmHg, respiratory rate 24 /min, SpO2 97% on room air, temperature 39.0 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: pain out of proportion, dusky discolouration of skin lateral calf, tenderness beyond erythema margin, no crepitations
data_table.Investigations: WCC 22.4 x10^9/L; haemoglobin 130 g/L (130 to 170); sodium 132 mmol/L; creatinine 142 micromol/L (baseline 80); CRP 240 mg/L; glucose 18.4 mmol/L; lactate 4.8 mmol/L; LRINEC score 9

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Urgent surgical consultation for immediate operative debridement after blood cultures and IV antibiotics
options.rationale: Pain out of proportion, dusky skin, tenderness beyond erythema, septic physiology, and LRINEC of 9 (high risk, more than 6) make necrotising fasciitis the working diagnosis. Survival depends on time to surgical debridement, which is the definitive treatment. Empirical IV antibiotics (meropenem plus vancomycin plus clindamycin under Therapeutic Guidelines) and resuscitation are given concurrently. CT or MRI must not delay theatre. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Mark the erythema with a marker pen and review in 4 hours on IV flucloxacillin
options.rationale: Outlining cellulitis and watching is appropriate for uncomplicated cellulitis without sepsis or red flags. The features here mandate operative assessment, not observation. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: CT with contrast of the leg before any surgical referral to confirm the diagnosis
options.rationale: Imaging supports diagnosis but must not delay theatre when clinical suspicion is high. CT or MRI may show gas or fascial fluid but a normal scan does not exclude necrotising fasciitis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Hyperbaric oxygen therapy in addition to IV piperacillin-tazobactam, deferring surgery
options.rationale: Hyperbaric oxygen is an adjunct considered only after surgical debridement. It does not replace operative source control. Piperacillin-tazobactam alone misses the clindamycin role in suppressing exotoxin production. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Trial of IV flucloxacillin 2 g six hourly for 24 hours and reassess for debridement
options.rationale: Monotherapy with flucloxacillin and observation in the setting of suspected necrotising fasciitis delays definitive care and increases mortality. The patient needs immediate broad-spectrum cover and theatre. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Necrotising fasciitis is a surgical emergency with mortality strongly correlated with time to debridement. Clinical clues are pain out of proportion to skin findings, dusky or purpura-like skin, crepitations, tenderness beyond erythema, rapid progression, and systemic toxicity (septic shock, AKI). The LRINEC score uses CRP, WCC, haemoglobin, sodium, creatinine, and glucose; a score of 6 or higher is high risk and 8 or higher is very high risk. Empirical antibiotics in Australia are meropenem plus vancomycin plus clindamycin (clindamycin for exotoxin suppression in group A streptococcal cases). Imaging must not delay theatre.
key_points: Time to surgical debridement is the strongest mortality determinant
Empirical antibiotics: meropenem plus vancomycin plus clindamycin
LRINEC 6 or higher is high risk; 8 or higher is very high risk
Imaging must not delay theatre
explanation.pearls: Necrotising fasciitis: immediate surgical debridement plus IV meropenem + vancomycin + clindamycin (clindamycin for exotoxin suppression). LRINEC 6 or higher is high risk. Do not delay theatre for imaging.

stem:
A 64-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by ambulance with sudden-onset severe breathlessness and one episode of syncope at home 30 minutes ago. She returned from a long-haul flight three days ago. She has no past medical history of bleeding, no recent surgery, and is not on anticoagulants.

On arrival she is pale, cool peripherally, and clammy. The right ventricle is felt heaving on palpation of the lower sternum. Bedside echocardiography shows a dilated and hypokinetic right ventricle with septal flattening. CT pulmonary angiogram demonstrates bilateral central filling defects. There is no evidence of intracranial pathology on CT brain and no history of recent stroke, surgery, or bleeding.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 132 /min, blood pressure 78/48 mmHg, respiratory rate 32 /min, SpO2 88% on 15 L non-rebreather, temperature 36.6 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: cool peripheries, prominent neck veins, loud P2, no calf swelling, GCS 15
data_table.Investigations: troponin I 220 ng/L (less than 14); BNP 1400 pg/mL; lactate 4.2 mmol/L; CTPA: bilateral central PE; echocardiography: dilated hypokinetic RV

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Emergent surgical pulmonary embolectomy as the first-line reperfusion strategy
options.rationale: Surgical embolectomy is reserved for patients with contraindications to thrombolysis or failed thrombolysis, and where cardiac surgical capability is on-site. Systemic thrombolysis remains first-line where not contraindicated. (Source: ESC PE Guidelines)
source_refs: ESC Guidelines for Acute Pulmonary Embolism

options.text: Therapeutic enoxaparin 1.5 mg/kg subcutaneously once daily and observe
options.rationale: Therapeutic enoxaparin alone is appropriate for non-massive PE. With shock and RV failure, the patient needs reperfusion therapy; anticoagulation alone has high mortality. (Source: ESC PE Guidelines; Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)
source_refs: ESC Guidelines for Acute Pulmonary Embolism

options.text: Apixaban 10 mg twice daily for 7 days then 5 mg twice daily for outpatient management
options.rationale: DOAC monotherapy is appropriate for intermediate-low or low-risk PE in haemodynamically stable patients suitable for outpatient care. Shock and RV dysfunction exclude this pathway. (Source: ESC PE Guidelines)
source_refs: ESC Guidelines for Acute Pulmonary Embolism

options.text: IV alteplase 100 mg over 2 hours alongside resuscitation and unfractionated heparin
options.rationale: Massive (high-risk) pulmonary embolism with sustained hypotension and right heart dysfunction has high short-term mortality. Australian and ESC guidelines recommend systemic thrombolysis (alteplase 100 mg over 2 hours, or 0.6 mg/kg over 15 minutes in cardiac arrest) when there are no absolute contraindications. Catheter-directed thrombolysis is an alternative in centres where it is available. (Source: ESC Guidelines for Acute Pulmonary Embolism; Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)
source_refs: ESC Guidelines for Acute Pulmonary Embolism

options.text: Inferior vena cava filter insertion before starting any anticoagulation
options.rationale: IVC filters are considered when anticoagulation is absolutely contraindicated or when recurrent PE occurs despite anticoagulation. They do not address the existing clot burden and provide no haemodynamic benefit. (Source: ESC PE Guidelines)
source_refs: ESC Guidelines for Acute Pulmonary Embolism

explanation.summary: Massive (high-risk) pulmonary embolism is defined by sustained hypotension (systolic BP under 90 mmHg for at least 15 minutes or needing vasopressors) or shock not from another cause. Mortality without reperfusion is high. ESC and Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular recommend systemic thrombolysis (alteplase 100 mg IV over 2 hours, or a weight-based bolus during cardiac arrest) with unfractionated heparin if there are no absolute contraindications (active intracranial pathology, recent ischaemic stroke, active bleeding). Catheter-directed thrombolysis and surgical embolectomy are alternatives. Anticoagulation alone is insufficient.
key_points: Massive PE: shock or sustained hypotension warrants reperfusion therapy
Alteplase 100 mg over 2 hours plus unfractionated heparin is the standard regimen
Catheter-directed thrombolysis is an alternative where available
Surgical embolectomy if thrombolysis contraindicated or failed
explanation.pearls: Massive PE with shock: IV alteplase 100 mg over 2 hours plus unfractionated heparin if no absolute contraindication. Catheter-directed thrombolysis or surgical embolectomy when contraindications or failure. Anticoagulation alone is insufficient.

sources.label: ESC Guidelines for Acute Pulmonary Embolism

stem:
A 42-year-old woman with autoimmune Addison's disease is brought to the emergency department by ambulance with vomiting, abdominal pain, lethargy, and confusion. She has been on hydrocortisone 20 mg in the morning and 10 mg at lunchtime with fludrocortisone 100 micrograms daily for 8 years. She has had a viral gastroenteritis for two days and stopped her hydrocortisone yesterday because she could not keep tablets down.

She is drowsy on arrival and disoriented to time and place. Her abdomen is diffusely tender without peritonism. She has been given 1 L of IV 0.9% sodium chloride en route. The medical registrar is at the bedside.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 124 /min, blood pressure 84/52 mmHg, respiratory rate 22 /min, SpO2 98% on room air, temperature 37.4 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: drowsy, dehydrated, abdomen mildly tender without peritonism, hyperpigmentation of buccal mucosa
data_table.Investigations: sodium 126 mmol/L (135 to 145); potassium 5.8 mmol/L (3.5 to 5.0); glucose 3.2 mmol/L (3.9 to 5.4); urea 12 mmol/L; creatinine 110 micromol/L (45 to 90)

lead_in: What is the single best immediate management step?

options.text: Oral hydrocortisone 20 mg stat once her vomiting has fully settled
options.rationale: Oral hydrocortisone is inappropriate in a vomiting drowsy patient; absorption is unreliable and the patient cannot keep it down. IV is the route in crisis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

options.text: IV dexamethasone 4 mg as a single dose while awaiting cortisol and ACTH results
options.rationale: Dexamethasone is used when diagnostic short Synacthen testing is planned and cortisol assay must not be confounded, in patients without an established diagnosis. In an established Addison's patient with a clear crisis, hydrocortisone is preferred for its mineralocorticoid activity. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

options.text: IV hydrocortisone 100 mg as a bolus then 200 mg per 24 hours as a continuous infusion
options.rationale: Adrenal crisis is a life-threatening glucocorticoid deficiency state. Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology and the Endocrine Society direct immediate IV hydrocortisone 100 mg bolus, then 200 mg per 24 hours as a continuous infusion (or 50 mg six hourly), plus IV fluid resuscitation and dextrose for hypoglycaemia. Bolus hydrocortisone at this dose has sufficient mineralocorticoid effect; separate fludrocortisone is unnecessary acutely. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology; Endocrine Society Adrenal Insufficiency Guideline)

options.text: IV fludrocortisone 200 micrograms with IV 0.9% sodium chloride bolus
options.rationale: Fludrocortisone is not available as an IV preparation in Australia. Hydrocortisone at high dose provides mineralocorticoid activity that adequately covers the acute phase. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

options.text: Withhold steroids until a short Synacthen test confirms adrenal insufficiency
options.rationale: Adrenal crisis is treated empirically. Withholding glucocorticoid pending diagnostic testing in an established or suspected crisis is associated with mortality. Diagnostic testing is performed later, on stable dose. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

explanation.summary: Adrenal crisis presents with hypotension, hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia, hypoglycaemia, and altered mental status, typically precipitated by a stress event (infection, surgery, trauma) in a patient with adrenal insufficiency. Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology directs immediate IV hydrocortisone 100 mg bolus followed by 200 mg per 24 hours by continuous infusion (or 50 mg six hourly), IV crystalloid resuscitation (1 L of 0.9% sodium chloride in the first hour, then guided by haemodynamics), and IV dextrose for hypoglycaemia. Always treat empirically; diagnostic short Synacthen testing waits for stability. Sick-day rules and emergency hydrocortisone injection kits prevent crises in known patients.
key_points: IV hydrocortisone 100 mg bolus then 200 mg per 24 hours by continuous infusion (or 50 mg six hourly)
IV 0.9% sodium chloride resuscitation; IV dextrose for hypoglycaemia
Empirical treatment; do not wait for short Synacthen testing in crisis
Sick-day rules and emergency injection kit prevent crisis in known patients
explanation.pearls: Adrenal crisis: IV hydrocortisone 100 mg bolus then 200 mg per 24 hours by infusion (or 50 mg six hourly). IV 0.9% saline resuscitation and IV dextrose for hypoglycaemia. Empirical treatment; do not wait for Synacthen testing.

stem:
A 58-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by ambulance with one hour of central crushing chest pain radiating to the jaw. He has hypertension and is a current smoker. Paramedics gave him aspirin 300 mg, oxygen, and sublingual GTN with one episode of profound hypotension after the GTN; his BP recovered with a 500 mL saline bolus.

On arrival he is pale and clammy but alert. The ECG shows 2 mm ST elevation in leads II, III, and aVF with reciprocal ST depression in I and aVL. A right-sided V4R lead shows 1.5 mm ST elevation. There is no murmur. Chest is clear and the JVP is elevated. Primary PCI is available on-site within 30 minutes.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 56 /min, blood pressure 92/62 mmHg, respiratory rate 20 /min, SpO2 96% on room air, temperature 36.6 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: elevated JVP, clear chest, no peripheral oedema, no murmurs
data_table.Investigations: ECG: 2 mm ST elevation II/III/aVF with reciprocal depression I/aVL; V4R 1.5 mm ST elevation; troponin I 380 ng/L (less than 14); chest film clear

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next intervention?

options.text: IV 0.9% sodium chloride 500 mL bolus and prepare for primary PCI
options.rationale: Inferior STEMI with right-sided V4R elevation indicates right ventricular involvement. RV infarcts are preload-dependent: nitrates and morphine are AVOIDED because they reduce preload and cause hypotension. Fluid loading restores RV output. Primary PCI within 90 minutes is the reperfusion strategy of choice. (Source: CSANZ Acute Coronary Syndrome Guidelines)

options.text: IV glyceryl trinitrate infusion starting at 5 micrograms per minute
options.rationale: Nitrates are contraindicated in RV infarction because they drop preload to the already volume-dependent right ventricle and can precipitate hypotension and cardiogenic shock. The first GTN dose already caused profound hypotension; further GTN would worsen this. (Source: CSANZ ACS Guidelines)

options.text: IV furosemide 40 mg now to offload the right ventricle today
options.rationale: Diuresis worsens RV infarct shock by reducing preload further. Furosemide is appropriate for LV failure with pulmonary oedema, which is not present here. (Source: CSANZ ACS Guidelines)

options.text: IV metoprolol 5 mg now before transfer to the cardiac catheter lab
options.rationale: Beta-blockers are deferred in haemodynamically unstable inferior STEMI, particularly with bradycardia and RV infarction, due to risk of cardiogenic shock and worsening AV block. They are introduced once stable. (Source: CSANZ ACS Guidelines)

options.text: Fibrinolysis with tenecteplase as the primary reperfusion strategy
options.rationale: Primary PCI is preferred over fibrinolysis when available within 90 minutes of first medical contact. Fibrinolysis is reserved for centres without timely access to PCI. (Source: CSANZ ACS Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Inferior STEMI carries a 30 to 50% risk of right ventricular involvement, especially when the proximal RCA is occluded. V4R lead with ST elevation of 1 mm or more confirms RV involvement. The right ventricle is preload-dependent, so RV infarction creates pseudo-cardiogenic shock that responds to fluid loading. Nitrates, morphine, and diuretics are avoided because they reduce preload. Primary PCI within 90 minutes of first medical contact is the reperfusion strategy of choice; fibrinolysis only if PCI not accessible within 120 minutes. Bradycardia and AV block are common in inferior infarcts and may need atropine or temporary pacing.
key_points: RV infarction is preload-dependent: load fluid, avoid nitrates, morphine, and diuretics
V4R ST elevation 1 mm or more confirms RV involvement
Primary PCI within 90 minutes is preferred over fibrinolysis
Bradycardia and AV block are common; atropine or temporary pacing as needed
explanation.pearls: Inferior STEMI with V4R ST elevation: RV infarct is preload-dependent. Fluid load, AVOID nitrates/morphine/diuretics, refer for primary PCI within 90 minutes. Be ready for bradyarrhythmia.
